3A_Bibliographic.citation: Nagata, J. M., Zamora, G., Al-Shoaibi, A. A., Lavender, J. M., Ganson, K. T., Testa, A., He, J., & Baker, F. C. (2025). Screen time and manic symptoms in early adolescents: prospective findings from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study. Social Psychiatry and Psychiatric Epidemiology, 60, 1479-1487.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Out of 11,875 participants, 1,828 had missing data for the manic symptoms measure in Year 3, and 804 had missing data for covariates in Year 1, leaving a sample size of 9,243 participants for this study.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Typical daily screen time (total and by subtype: TV/movies, videos, video games, texting, video chat, social media) at Year 1
3C_Exposure.modalities: total screen time, television shows/movies, videos (YouTube), video games, texting, video chat, social media
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous hours/day (weighted average of weekday and weekend)
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Screen time was obtained from the annual data of the ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey. Adolescents answered questions about daily time spent on six different screen use subtypes (viewing/streaming TV shows or movies, watching/streaming videos, playing video games, texting, video chatting, and social media). Typical screen time was calculated using the following formula: [(weekday average x 5) + (weekend average x 2)]/7.

3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: 7 Up subscale (7 items) of the 7 Up 7 Down Inventory
3D_Outcome.base_rate_if_binary: N/A - continuous outcome (clinical cutoff >= 11 used in sensitivity analyses: 0.8% at Year 3)
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: The ABCD study used the 7 Up subscale (7 items) of the 7 Up 7 Down Inventory to assess the presence of manic/hypomanic symptoms among individuals. Adolescents responded to questions about their behaviors using a scale from (0=Never) to (3=very often or almost constantly). Items were summed to create a total score. Internal consistency was good for the 7 Up subscale (alpha=0.77).

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Multivariable linear regression with robust standard errors; also mediation via generalized structural equation models with maximum likelihood estimation
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parent education, study site, manic symptoms in Year 1, ADHD symptoms in Year 1, depressive symptoms in Year 1, data collection timeframe (pre-COVID vs during COVID)
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: We assessed the associations of typical daily screen time with manic symptoms using multivariable linear regression models with robust standard error. Model 2 was adjusted for age, sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parent education, data collection site, manic symptoms in Year 1, ADHD symptoms in Year 1, depressive symptoms in Year 1, and data collection timeframe. Two-sided p<0.05 was considered to indicate statistical significance.

3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Overall screen time: B=0.05, 95% CI 0.03, 0.07, p<0.001

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Social media (Year 1) -> Problematic social media use (Year 2) -> Manic symptoms (Year 3), indirect effect
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Indirect effect, B=0.12, 95% CI 0.09, 0.16, p<0.001 (47.7% mediation)

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Video games (Year 1) -> Problematic video game use (Year 2) -> Manic symptoms (Year 3), indirect effect
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Indirect effect, B=0.08, 95% CI 0.05, 0.10, p<0.001 (58.0% mediation)

3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: The effect size for overall typical daily screen time was relatively small. However, total effects are greater with more hours per day of screen exposure, and cumulative exposure to screens over several years may yield stronger associations.
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Paper acknowledges small effect for total screen time (B=0.05 per hour/day on a 0-21 scale) but then argues cumulative exposure may yield stronger associations and proceeds to recommend prevention/intervention programs.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Healthcare professionals could also consider advising limits on video games, social media use, and other potentially problematic digital engagement for adolescents with mental health concerns, including manic symptoms. One such example is the implementation of digital literacy classes in grade schools.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: Problematic screen use may be a target for mental health prevention and early intervention efforts among adolescents.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.per_unit_verbatim: total effects are greater with more hours per day of screen exposure, and cumulative exposure to screens over several years may yield stronger associations
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion acknowledges the effect size for overall screen time is relatively small but argues cumulative and per-unit effects may be larger. It focuses on neurobiological mechanisms (reward pathways, dopamine) to explain how screen time could cause manic symptoms, and recommends targeted prevention and intervention programs including digital literacy classes and clinical advice to limit screens. The paper uses language suggesting screen time is a modifiable risk factor for mania despite the observational design and small effect sizes.
